programme.title: The National
programme.desc: CBC's flagship nightly news program, featuring the day's top stories with in-depth and original journalism; Adrienne Arsenault and Ian Hanomansing anchor.

programme.desc: The latest news in Canada and around the world.

programme.desc: Andrew Chang expands the understanding of the stories everybody's talking about.

programme.desc: What's happening across Canada and around the world.

programme.title: CBC News: Morning
programme.desc: What do you need to know about the world around you? Heather Hiscox takes the helm as the anchor for this weekday-morning news program to tell you. Joining her is Colleen Jones with weather and sports info., and Danielle Bochove with all you need to know about the business world.

programme.desc: Andrew Chang expands the understanding of the stories everybody's talking about.

programme.title: Question Period
programme.desc: Question Period, the national political landmark program, is a must-see for political junkies.

programme.title: CTV News Special: Rick Hansen: Unbreakable 50 Years Later
programme.desc: Rick Hansen returns to the scene of the accident that changed his life forever. CTV’s Sandie Rinaldo talks to the Canadian icon.

programme.title: W5: Avery Haines Investigates
programme.desc: Avery rides atop Mexico’s “death train” through narco territory, documenting the harrowing journey migrants take in search of a better life.

programme.title: CTV News Special: Rick Hansen: Unbreakable 50 Years Later
programme.desc: Rick Hansen returns to the scene of the accident that changed his life forever. CTV’s Sandie Rinaldo talks to the Canadian icon.

programme.desc: Question Period, the national political landmark program, is a must-see for political junkies.

programme.desc: Stories that showcase what makes Canada unique.

programme.desc: An investigator uses new scientific advances to track a 16-year-old girl's murderer, 50 years later.

programme.title: Holmes Family Effect
programme.desc: The Holmes family helps transform a high school and its skilled trades program, with some help from school alumnus and guest Russell Peters.

programme.title: Cross Country Cake Off
programme.desc: Bakers from the West present their best cakes to judges Mary Berg and Andrew Han. The top four bake Outdoor Adventure cakes to continue on.

programme.title: Cross Country Cake Off
programme.desc: The Prairies’ top cake makers meet the judges in Edmonton and four are chosen to bring a “Water World” to life for a spot in the finale.

programme.title: Cross Country Cake Off
programme.desc: Mid-Canada’s cake makers present their edible art. The best move on to bake a “Child’s Play” cake in the Cross Country Cake Off kitchen.

programme.title: Cross Country Cake Off
programme.desc: Mary and Andrew rendezvous in Montreal to taste eight Quebecers’ cakes. The top four bake “After Dark” cakes for a spot in the finale.

programme.title: Cross Country Cake Off
programme.desc: The qualifying round ends in the East with eight Atlantic cake makers. Four are chosen to bake their “Bucket List” cake for the judges.

programme.title: Flour Power
programme.desc: Host Jessica McGovern shows us how to impress both kids and adults with three over-the-top desserts

programme.title: Flour Power
programme.desc: Baker Jessica McGovern offers tips on how to prepare adorable treats for any shower party.

programme.title: Flour Power
programme.sub-title: Desserts You Can Eat with Your Fingers
programme.desc: Host Jessica McGovern shows us how to make glorious desserts that you can eat with your fingers!

programme.title: Flour Power
programme.desc: Baking expert Jessica McGovern demonstrates the versatility of this wonder ingredient.

programme.title: Flour Power
programme.desc: Host Jessica McGovern bakes up do-it-yourself recipes that will spice up any wedding.

programme.title: Flour Power
programme.desc: Host Jessica McGovern creates worldly desserts.

programme.title: Flour Power
programme.desc: Host Jessica McGovern shows off some of her favourite picnic-approved desserts.

programme.title: Flour Power
programme.desc: Jessica creates breakfast treats.

programme.title: Flour Power
programme.desc: Surprise! Host Jessica McGovern uses surprising ingredients in three delicious desserts.

programme.title: Voyage of the Continents
programme.sub-title: Asia: Rising Mountains and Sinking Countries
programme.desc: As the Indo-Asian collision continued into the rest of Asia, the deforming pressure created islands.

programme.title: Voyage of the Continents
programme.desc: One billion years ago there was just one great continent on earth. Then, it began to break apart.

programme.title: Order and Disorder
programme.desc: Jim investigates a strange set of laws that link together everything from engines to humans to stars.

programme.title: Order and Disorder
programme.desc: Jim learns that information isn't just about human communication; it's woven into the very fabric of reality.

programme.title: The Age of Hubble
programme.desc: The Hubble Space Telescope has delivered unprecedented discoveries about the nature of our Universe, and more.

programme.title: Dust Storm
programme.desc: We investigate dust storm causes, and how to predict them and lessen their impact.

programme.title: Solar Superstorms: Journey to the Center of the Sun
programme.desc: As technology and electrical grids have spread, big solar storms pose a threat equal to the worst hurricanes.

programme.title: Asteroid Mining: The New El Dorado
programme.desc: Our recent rise in consumption has fueled a need for rare metals, but where can we find enough?

programme.title: China's Long March Into Space
programme.desc: Three men sit atop 500 tons of liquid fuel in China's Shenzhou 7 ship, ready to blast 185 miles into space.

programme.title: The Jupiter Enigma
programme.desc: NASA’s Juno mission to Jupiter is revealing the origins of our solar system, when Earth's fate was uncertain.

programme.title: Our Roots, Our Future
programme.desc: Scientists are studying root growth. Roots may help solve climate change and protect the environment.

programme.title: Voyage of the Continents
programme.desc: Over time, the Atlantic Ocean widened. Then Africa moved north, struck Europe and birthed the Alps.

programme.title: Voyage of the Continents
programme.desc: Gondwana, the first, single, landmass broke apart into today’s continents, but its heart was Africa.

programme.title: Voyage of the Continents
programme.desc: Volcanic activity and climate change define Africa’s modern history.

programme.title: Disasters in Space
programme.desc: Relive the harrowing moments in Launch Control during NASA's second "Return to Flight" test flight .

programme.title: Disasters in Space
programme.desc: As the clock ticks toward the critical Mars launch window, NASA scrambles to fix a critical space mistake.

programme.title: Disasters in Space
programme.desc: Despite multiple pressures, a shocking discovery causes NASA to cancel a historic launch.

programme.title: Disasters in Space
programme.desc: A spacecraft freefalls from orbit smashing into the ground when its parachute fails to deploy.

programme.title: Disasters in Space
programme.desc: A harrowing space mistake leaves the solid rocket boosters hanging by a thread.

programme.title: Disasters in Space
programme.desc: The loss of Space Shuttle Columbia stands as one of the worst disasters in spaceflight history.

programme.title: China's Long March Into Space
programme.desc: Three men sit atop 500 tons of liquid fuel in China's Shenzhou 7 ship, ready to blast 185 miles into space.

programme.title: The Saturn V Story
programme.desc: This is the story of the most powerful machine ever built and a nation who watched on with hope, together.

programme.title: Faster Than Light: the Dream of Interstellar Flight
programme.desc: Next-generation rocket technology is fueling the dream of traveling across the void.

programme.title: Our World is on Fire
programme.desc: Climate change is now a crisis, and our world is crying out. Examine some possible remedies to the emergency.

programme.title: Day of the Asteroid
programme.desc: Armed with new defensive technologies, scientists are getting ready for the Day of the Asteroid.

programme.title: Searching for Planet 9
programme.desc: Is there a "Planet 9" five times more massive than planet Earth? There's fascinating new research out there.

programme.title: Hubble: Eye in the Sky
programme.desc: In this beautiful show, viewers get an inside look at the challenges of operating the Hubble telescope.

programme.title: The Age of Hubble
programme.desc: The Hubble Space Telescope has delivered unprecedented discoveries about the nature of our Universe, and more.

programme.title: Mega Disaster
programme.desc: Volcanoes can bury entire cities in clouds of ash and gas, or turn rivers into torrents of mud and debris.

programme.title: Mega Disaster
programme.desc: There is no stopping these massive and deadly phenomena, the only thing to do is run to higher ground.

programme.title: Mega Disaster
programme.desc: From the tremendous, to the terrifying, to the tragic, this is a countdown to a mega tornado.

programme.title: Mega Disaster
programme.desc: Earthquakes baffle experts as they try to gather the information necessary to predict the next big one.

programme.title: Earth in 1000 years
programme.desc: Earth's stores of ice on Greenland and Antarctica are melting, and sea levels rising. Where is Earth headed?

programme.title: Antarctica
programme.desc: Explore Antarctica, one of the world's most beautiful, yet inhospitable, landscapes.

programme.title: Facing Forces of Nature
programme.desc: An asteroid could be barreling toward Earth at any time, now or in the future. Only science can save us.

programme.title: Facing Forces of Nature
programme.desc: Tornadoes: Can we use modern technology to predict the emergence and path of this violent force of nature?

programme.title: Facing Forces of Nature
programme.desc: Volcanoes: How successful can we be in the fight against this violent, destructive, elemental force of nature?

programme.title: Facing Forces of Nature
programme.desc: Melting ice at Earth's poles: Do we still have a chance to stop it from melting and prevent its repercussions?

programme.title: Wild Faces of Switzerland
programme.desc: Take an exciting journey of discovery into an almost untouched wilderness: The Jura Mountains.

programme.title: Wild Faces of Switzerland
programme.desc: This episode focuses on Ticino, reaching from the Tessin Alps down to the charming Muggio Valley.

programme.title: Wild Faces of Switzerland
programme.desc: Take an amazing journey from the highest peaks of the Bernese Alps to a spectacular finish at the Rhine Falls.

programme.title: Wild Faces of Switzerland
programme.desc: The ibex, marmot, and European adder have all developed strategies to survive the harsh alpine winter.

programme.title: Fascinating Places
programme.desc: The West Coast of Madagascar is remote and inhospitable but home to an astonishing array of animals.

programme.title: Fascinating Places
programme.desc: The Northeast of Madagascar is a major trading center and one of the most species-rich regions in the world.

programme.title: Fascinating Places
programme.desc: Madagascar is an island paradise in the Indian Ocean, home to fun-loving folk and a unique natural landscape.

programme.title: Fascinating Places
programme.desc: The Black Forest captivates all who visit and has a reputation for its unique landscape and inhabitants.

programme.title: Fascinating Places
programme.sub-title: Wilderness In the Heart of Europe
programme.desc: The Vosges are home to countless species, encompassing mountains, valleys, forests, and fields.

programme.title: Fascinating Places
programme.sub-title: In the Valley of the Storks
programme.desc: The emblem of The Vosges is the stork. Once gone almost entirely, there are now 400 resident breeding pairs.

programme.title: Fascinating Places
programme.sub-title: On Safari in Kenya’s Nature Paradise
programme.desc: The Maasai Mara is one of the most breathtaking animal habitats in the world.

programme.title: Fascinating Places
programme.desc: There are few places on earth that are closer to the stars than the high Andes.

programme.title: Fascinating Places
programme.desc: Patagonia seems to be primarily uninhabited by man, which makes this harsh, wild land a paradise for animals.

programme.title: Fascinating Places
programme.sub-title: From Pantanal to the Iguacu Waterfalls
programme.desc: The Pantanal, in the southwest of Brazil, is the largest marsh in the world.

programme.title: Super Senses: Wild Feats of Evolution
programme.desc: The human imagination is limited when it comes to animal senses.

programme.title: Super Senses: Wild Feats of Evolution
programme.desc: On this episode we travel from the desert to the forest to learn how creatures never miss an auditory trick.

programme.title: Super Senses: Wild Feats of Evolution
programme.desc: On this episode of Super Senses, we get in touch with the feelings of wild animals.

programme.title: Super Senses: Wild Feats of Evolution
programme.desc: On this episode we examine how things taste, and how vital scent is from the animal perspective.

programme.title: Super Senses: Wild Feats of Evolution
programme.desc: There are senses we share with most animals, but what about those senses that humans aren't equipped with?

programme.title: Super Senses: Wild Feats of Evolution
programme.desc: There’s a whole world of sensations that animals are aware of but humans are completely unable to detect.

programme.title: Super Senses: Wild Feats of Evolution
programme.desc: In this episode, we examine how every animal has what could be called a sense of rhythm.

programme.title: Super Senses: Wild Feats of Evolution
programme.desc: While all life began in the oceans, human beings now rely on technology to navigate the seven seas.

programme.title: Super Senses: Wild Feats of Evolution
programme.desc: While all life began in the oceans, human beings now rely on technology to navigate the seven seas.

programme.title: Super Senses: Wild Feats of Evolution
programme.desc: In this episode we find out more about the animals that live in the shadows.

programme.title: Earth's Survival: Decoding the Science
programme.desc: This documentary explains how we may be in the middle of the most crucial moment of Earth’s history.

programme.title: Fascinating Places
programme.desc: The West Coast of Madagascar is remote and inhospitable but home to an astonishing array of animals.

programme.title: Fascinating Places
programme.desc: The Northeast of Madagascar is a major trading center and one of the most species-rich regions in the world.

programme.title: Fascinating Places
programme.desc: Madagascar is an island paradise in the Indian Ocean, home to fun-loving folk and a unique natural landscape.

programme.desc: From the earth to the stars to the edge of the unknown, explore the mystery, majesty and power of the world we live in and beyond.

programme.title: Evolution Earth
programme.desc: From the earth to the stars to the edge of the unknown, explore the mystery, majesty and power of the world we live in and beyond.

programme.title: The Secret world of Herbs
programme.sub-title: The Secret World of Herbs - In the Alps
programme.desc: Secluded for many centuries in inaccessible valleys, the inhabitants of the Alps had no alternative but to rely on the healing powers of nature. A knowledge of herbs was deeply rooted in these mountain-dwellers’ everyday lives – until the twentieth century, when this wisdom became eclipsed by modern academic medicine. Today, Alpine herbs are witnessing a revival, as public interest in regional natural treasures grows. The potential of Alpine herbs is far from exhausted. In the Berchtesgaden Alps, the film shows Hubsi Ilsanker at work as a root-digger, a back-breaking job that has scarcely changed in four hundred years. Authorized by a special historic permit to supply the oldest gentian distillery in Germany, Hubsi digs for protected gentian roots in Berchtesgaden National Park. For yellow gentian is one of the most bitter medicinal herbs in the world, well suited for schnapps and as a general tonic.

programme.title: The Secret world of Herbs
programme.sub-title: The Secret world of Herbs - In the Balkans
programme.desc: In the pristine nature and mild climate of southeastern Europe grows a unique diversity of wild plants. Far removed from major industry and polluted soils, the Balkan countries have become the largest exporter of herbs in Europe. In Bulgaria alone, more than 300,000 people work with domestic plants. The business is becoming increasingly lucrative. The film takes us to the Croatian coast. On the Adriatic island of Cres, the audience meets Mladen Dragoslavić. In May, when the sage begins to blossom, this beekeeper has one month to produce his income for an entire year – always with the goal of creating the best sage honey in the Balkans. Given a choice, the bees would never choose sage, for entering and exiting the narrow blossom is strenuous. Within three weeks, maximum, Mladen must remove his bees from the island. Otherwise they will die of exhaustion. In Skopje, the capital of Macedonia, the film visits Iva Yosifova. Hollyhock provides the raw material for her objects of art. The herb is medically beneficial, due to its mucilage, which is added to many cough tea mixtures. Iva, though, makes paper from hollyhock. Thanks to the herb’s long fibres, the paper can be spread onto objects and modeled. The technique produces delicate sculptures, as fragile as nature.

programme.title: Bavaria's Alpine Kingdom
programme.desc: There are sparkling palaces from ice and snow, like those found in a fairytale. The icy castles are the phenomenal work of Mother Nature but man had a hand in the magnificent stone castles of the region. The spectacular and histrionic mountains of the Bavarian Alps challenge wildlife and the people who live there and the rocky ramparts of this mountain kingdom are built of limestone and fossil rocks that tell of its origins beneath the sea. Millions of visitors from all over the world come to Bavaria every year to witness the mysticism of the landscape. Kings and Queens, tourist and locals, all are welcomed in the fantastic alpine kingdom. Early summer often comes with the threat of storms that bring floods that scour the land and threaten many creatures. But farmers risk these dangers to take their herds to the highest pastures, where they can graze on the sweetest flower-filled meadows. And later from the high-quality milk produced by their herds, the famers make highly prized cheese. Humankind lives among eagle owls, chamoix, wild cats, stoats and ravens up in these mountains.

programme.title: Chiemgau, A Changing Landscape
programme.desc: A few thousand years ago, there were bare moraine ridges and barren meltwater valleys here. A landscape born in the last ice age, characterized by the edge of the Alps, gentle hills, moors, and lakes. And by cultural richness, not only on the islands in Lake Chiemsee, with their famous monasteries and castles. Long before King Ludwig II, the first people settled in the Chiemgau region. Finds of hunting weapons from the Neanderthal era prove that the land was inhabited even during the ice age, probably because there was plenty of game to hunt. During the Bronze Age, one of the most important trade routes from the mountains to the Alpine foothills passed through here. Then came the Celts and the Romans.

programme.title: Chiemgau, A Changing Landscape
programme.desc: Three times in the past two decades, the Chiemsee area has experienced "once-in-a-century" floods. Climate, wildlife, and landscape have changed, and it seems this transformation is accelerating. Species living in the flood zones are developing sophisticated survival strategies. Knot ants, for instance, place their queen in the middle and form a raft with their bodies, even conquering new habitats. Flooding is less of a disaster for nature and impacts humans more significantly.

programme.title: The marvellous World of French Rivers
programme.desc: The Gironde is the largest and most powerful tidal current in Europe. When the Gironde flows into the Atlantic, it is 15 kilometers wide and in the rhythm of the tides, it brings the waters from the Pyrenees and the Massif Central into the ocean. This often pushes its waters far out into the Gironde at high tide.This creates a world of its own that has shaped everything uniquely and intensely for tens of thousands of years. The water surface of the river is so large that the evaporation has created its own microclimate, which is stable and, together with the brackish waters, has influenced flora and fauna on the right and left of the bank for a long time.

programme.title: The marvellous World of French Rivers
programme.desc: The Dordogne consists of a visible and an invisible system of flowing water. Caves and basins under the earth's surface store the excess water and return it to the river when it is dry. A perfect management of a precious resource, not invented by humans, but by the complex nature itself. The result is an ever life-giving river.

programme.title: One Year in Norway's Lofoten
programme.sub-title: One Year in Norway's Lofoten: Episode 1
programme.desc: Winter means more than just the beginning of the year for the people of Lofoten: For more than a thousand years, Lofoten has been a hub for cod fishing, with seasons lasting from February to April as huge shoals migrate from the Barents Sea to these waters. During these hectic months, more than 65,000 tons of fish are brought to shore, securing the income for the whole of Lofoten for years to come. Around a quarter of the fish caught ends up on lines to dry upon racks. From a young age, the children from the fishing villages get to earn some pocket money by cutting cod tongues and selling the produce as a delicacy appreciated by the locals.

programme.title: One Year in Norway's Lofoten
programme.sub-title: One Year in Norway's Lofoten: Episode 2
programme.desc: Almost unnoticeably, spring turns into summer. The days become longer and longer, with a never-setting sun. People who have spent their winter and spring working hard can finally enjoy their own nature, even though the temperature is only slightly warmer. Lofoten is sparsely populated, just 24,000 people live here. Unstad is one of its smallest communities with only 15 inhabitants, but despite that, it has become a hotspot for arctic surf and is host to the northern-most surf center in the world. Surfers from all over the globe come to surf in the arctic water.

programme.title: Secrets of the Mediterranean
programme.desc: The contours of the Mediterranean have changed during the course of its history. Near Gibraltar, Africa was once connected to the European continent. When the land bridge at Gibraltar collapsed, the Mediterranean basin filled up again. The Suez Canal is today connected to the Red Sea. This route enables animals to reach the Mediterranean, where they cause problems.

programme.title: Secrets of the Mediterranean
programme.desc: Rugged rocks, blue sea and idyllic, sandy beaches - this is the Mediterranean as we know it. But this impression is deceptive: the Mediterranean is under constant change. The continents are moving towards one another. Animals, that have to survive under such extreme conditions, must find a way to escape the dangers. Between deserts and volcanoes, the challenges for the animals are enormous.

programme.title: The Secret world of Herbs
programme.sub-title: The Secret World of Herbs - In Provence
programme.desc: Its native fragrant flowers and aromatic herbs have made the Provence a brand known worldwide. The wealth of flora found in this region, between the French Alps and the Mediterranean, is unequalled among the French provinces. Around Sault, near Mont Ventoux, the valleys and high plains are carpeted with bright violet: In the hot climate of the South of France, lavender blooms and exudes its fragrance. Monastic healers have been using its aromatic medicinal oil since the Middle Ages. And today an entire industry is based on the trade with this fragrant flower. Yet the livelihood of Provençal lavender farmers is imperiled. In France’s major lavender-growing region, the “Plateau de Valensole”, our viewers encounter Gérard Blanc, who has been hit particularly hard. The lavender farmer lost sixty percent of his harvest to one tiny insect: the leafhopper. This insect has already destroyed half of the lavender acreage, bumping France from its market position as the world’s top producer of lavender oil. The film visits a resolute farmer who, not about to give up, is testing methods to fend off the voracious insect.

programme.title: The Secret world of Herbs
programme.sub-title: The Secret World of Herbs - In India
programme.desc: In India, herbs play an everyday role hardly equalled anywhere in the world. From the tropical green south to the mountains of the northern Himalayas, herbs lend fragrance to joss sticks and soaps, and flavor to the cuisine. And, above all, herbs heal. Six thousand varieties of plants are used in India, and most are harvested in the wild – where they are becoming depleted. The ever rising need for herbal medicine for the subcontinent’s 1.3 billion inhabitants comes on top of the demand of its growing middle class for herbal-based cosmetics. As a result, certain herbs have become scarce, while others have already vanished entirely. Most of the herbs in India are harvested in the wild. The progress of first cultivation attempts is slow. In India, the rural population, especially, needs affordable, well-tolerated medicine – medicine based on herbs. In Kerala, the film shows a family operation that hand-produces botanical medicine for the local market. High up in the Himalayas, we observe a village healer at work. The science of Ayurvedic medicine is five thousand years old. Today, interest in the ancient healing art is surging, not only in India. In Delhi’s Old Town, the film visits a wholesaler who supplies the booming global herb market, and we take a look inside the laboratories of the largest manufacturer of Ayurvedic medicines in the world. In Kerala, we meet a doctor employed at a small Ayurveda clinic, and in Nordern India, we join a student of Tibetan medicine on a hike up into the mountains, where she prepares for an exam in herbology.

programme.title: The Secret world of Herbs
programme.sub-title: The Secret World of Herbs - In Latin America
programme.desc: Healing effects, fragrance, and intense flavor – the benefits of herbs were discovered by the indigenous peoples of South America long before Spanish conquistadores subdued the continent. Even today, in the remote regions of South America, life without herbs is unthinkable. For many inhabitants, they provide the sole medicine. For more than two thousand years, this wisdom of the healing powers of nature has been passed down from generation to generation. In Bolivia, the film accompanies ethnobiologist Rainer Bussmann on one of his expeditions documenting which herbs the locals use against which illnesses. The German scientist has spent the last 15 years in the region, focussed on preserving this invaluable knowledge.

programme.title: The Secret world of Herbs
programme.sub-title: The Secret World of Herbs - In the Alps
programme.desc: Secluded for many centuries in inaccessible valleys, the inhabitants of the Alps had no alternative but to rely on the healing powers of nature. A knowledge of herbs was deeply rooted in these mountain-dwellers’ everyday lives – until the twentieth century, when this wisdom became eclipsed by modern academic medicine. Today, Alpine herbs are witnessing a revival, as public interest in regional natural treasures grows. The potential of Alpine herbs is far from exhausted. In the Berchtesgaden Alps, the film shows Hubsi Ilsanker at work as a root-digger, a back-breaking job that has scarcely changed in four hundred years. Authorized by a special historic permit to supply the oldest gentian distillery in Germany, Hubsi digs for protected gentian roots in Berchtesgaden National Park. For yellow gentian is one of the most bitter medicinal herbs in the world, well suited for schnapps and as a general tonic.

programme.title: The Secret world of Herbs
programme.sub-title: The Secret world of Herbs - In the Balkans
programme.desc: In the pristine nature and mild climate of southeastern Europe grows a unique diversity of wild plants. Far removed from major industry and polluted soils, the Balkan countries have become the largest exporter of herbs in Europe. In Bulgaria alone, more than 300,000 people work with domestic plants. The business is becoming increasingly lucrative. The film takes us to the Croatian coast. On the Adriatic island of Cres, the audience meets Mladen Dragoslavić. In May, when the sage begins to blossom, this beekeeper has one month to produce his income for an entire year – always with the goal of creating the best sage honey in the Balkans. Given a choice, the bees would never choose sage, for entering and exiting the narrow blossom is strenuous. Within three weeks, maximum, Mladen must remove his bees from the island. Otherwise they will die of exhaustion. In Skopje, the capital of Macedonia, the film visits Iva Yosifova. Hollyhock provides the raw material for her objects of art. The herb is medically beneficial, due to its mucilage, which is added to many cough tea mixtures. Iva, though, makes paper from hollyhock. Thanks to the herb’s long fibres, the paper can be spread onto objects and modeled. The technique produces delicate sculptures, as fragile as nature.

programme.title: Bavaria's Alpine Kingdom
programme.desc: There are sparkling palaces from ice and snow, like those found in a fairytale. The icy castles are the phenomenal work of Mother Nature but man had a hand in the magnificent stone castles of the region. The spectacular and histrionic mountains of the Bavarian Alps challenge wildlife and the people who live there and the rocky ramparts of this mountain kingdom are built of limestone and fossil rocks that tell of its origins beneath the sea. Millions of visitors from all over the world come to Bavaria every year to witness the mysticism of the landscape. Kings and Queens, tourist and locals, all are welcomed in the fantastic alpine kingdom. Early summer often comes with the threat of storms that bring floods that scour the land and threaten many creatures. But farmers risk these dangers to take their herds to the highest pastures, where they can graze on the sweetest flower-filled meadows. And later from the high-quality milk produced by their herds, the famers make highly prized cheese. Humankind lives among eagle owls, chamoix, wild cats, stoats and ravens up in these mountains.

programme.title: Chiemgau, A Changing Landscape
programme.desc: A few thousand years ago, there were bare moraine ridges and barren meltwater valleys here. A landscape born in the last ice age, characterized by the edge of the Alps, gentle hills, moors, and lakes. And by cultural richness, not only on the islands in Lake Chiemsee, with their famous monasteries and castles. Long before King Ludwig II, the first people settled in the Chiemgau region. Finds of hunting weapons from the Neanderthal era prove that the land was inhabited even during the ice age, probably because there was plenty of game to hunt. During the Bronze Age, one of the most important trade routes from the mountains to the Alpine foothills passed through here. Then came the Celts and the Romans.

programme.title: Chiemgau, A Changing Landscape
programme.desc: Three times in the past two decades, the Chiemsee area has experienced "once-in-a-century" floods. Climate, wildlife, and landscape have changed, and it seems this transformation is accelerating. Species living in the flood zones are developing sophisticated survival strategies. Knot ants, for instance, place their queen in the middle and form a raft with their bodies, even conquering new habitats. Flooding is less of a disaster for nature and impacts humans more significantly.

programme.title: The marvellous World of French Rivers
programme.desc: The Gironde is the largest and most powerful tidal current in Europe. When the Gironde flows into the Atlantic, it is 15 kilometers wide and in the rhythm of the tides, it brings the waters from the Pyrenees and the Massif Central into the ocean. This often pushes its waters far out into the Gironde at high tide.This creates a world of its own that has shaped everything uniquely and intensely for tens of thousands of years. The water surface of the river is so large that the evaporation has created its own microclimate, which is stable and, together with the brackish waters, has influenced flora and fauna on the right and left of the bank for a long time.

programme.title: The marvellous World of French Rivers
programme.desc: The Dordogne consists of a visible and an invisible system of flowing water. Caves and basins under the earth's surface store the excess water and return it to the river when it is dry. A perfect management of a precious resource, not invented by humans, but by the complex nature itself. The result is an ever life-giving river.

programme.title: Children of the Sun
programme.sub-title: Children of the Sun - Wild Bees
programme.desc: Most people associate honey producing insects with the name “BEE“. Few realize that there are over 560 different species of bees - most of them live their lives “busy as a bee“ but some “abuse“ other insects and others are even known to be murderers. The film “Wild Bees” shows the incredible diversity and the stunning adaptations of our “BEES” to various habitats and diverse living conditions. Fascinating fights, sensational performances, imposing structures and colorful individuals – the world of “Wild Bees” offers all of this and more.

programme.title: Children of the Sun
programme.desc: Butterflies surely are the most fancied species inside the insect world. Their beautiful colouring and their graceful flight appeal to everyone. There are close to 4000 types of Butterflies – today many of them are endangered due to the loss of their habitat.

programme.title: Missing Butterflies
programme.desc: Its miraculous transformation from caterpillar to remarkable winged beauty has been a point of constant intrigue. However, in the last few years, many have observed a decrease in the butterfly population. A much-discussed topic in the entomological society is the decline of the insect populace, but to what extent is this topic relevant to our beloved butterflies? For years, a teacher from lower Bavaria and a biologist from Munich have dedicated themselves to studying the butterfly and their presence in Bavaria. The results of their studies tell an enlightening story that is sure to entertain.

programme.title: Mantises - Masters of Deception
programme.desc: They are killers. They kill with unbelievable precision. They fight Kung Fu style and are seen in Japan as a symbol of vigilance - the mantises. Their triangular head with its unique flexibility is conspicuous. Two overdimensioned eyes fixate the distance to their prey rapidly and three-dimensionally. The chest segment of the mantis is prolonged and equipped with spiny appendages that can spear their prey as fast as a jack knife. The mysterious aura that surrounds the praying mantis has a lot to do with the fact that they are rarely seen. They have adapted to their surroundings perfectly. No matter whether leaves, blossoms, tree bark, sandy floors or even orchids - the mantis adjusts to all environments.

programme.title: Hummingbirds: Jewelled Messengers
programme.desc: Hummingbirds live at the limits of what is possible. They have the highest metabolism with a heart rate of 1,200 beats per minute of any vertebrate. Their wings beat 50 times a second. They can hover on the spot, and even fly backwards. To our eyes, they are just a blur. But with the aid of high-speed cameras we can slow down their hyperactive lives and look into their secret world.

programme.title: The Secret of Migrating Birds - Tryst at the Red Sea
programme.desc: Each year in spring, Israel witnesses a unique spectacle. Many millions of migrating birds cross the land bridge between Africa, Asia and Europe.Their first major roosting place after an exhausting nonstop flight over the African deserts is in southern Israel – a vital stopover. Here they replenish their energy reserves. The flocks of migrating birds are pursued by flocks of ornithologists. Hundreds of birders flood into the port city of Eilat on the Red Sea to take part in the Migration Festival. For a week, Israel's southernmost coastal resort is home to international scientists and amateur birders.

programme.title: Animal Armoury
programme.desc: The archer fish uses a blowgun to capture insects, while the jawfish defends itself by throwing boulders.

programme.title: Animal Armoury
programme.desc: Cuttlefish and squid use black fluid to hide from predators, while skunks also use scent glands for defense.

programme.title: Lions On The Move
programme.desc: Kevin Richardson, animal lover, faces the challenge of relocating 28 lions and 14 hyenas.

programme.title: The Unknown Snake Of Bolivia
programme.desc: Herpetologists Juan Tims and Hiram Cuevas explore Bolivia to find venomous snakes and a new viper.

programme.title: Masters Of The Wild
programme.desc: Animals adapt to hostile landscapes using specialized bodies, diets, and behaviors, such as scorpions

programme.title: Killer Whales - Up Close and Personal
programme.desc: Delphine, a young killer whale, navigates the Crozet Islands, learning to hunt penguins and sea creatures.

programme.title: Megeti
programme.desc: A lone Ethiopian Wolf battles the elements and other predators as she tries to unite with a pack.

programme.title: Wild Faces Of Switzerland
programme.desc: We visit the Jura Mountains, vast and mysterious

programme.title: Wild Faces Of Switzerland
programme.desc: Graubünden in winter means there is a struggle for survival because of the cold.

programme.title: Wild Italy: Top Predators
programme.desc: The wolf is performing a remarkable comeback in the mountains of Italy.

programme.title: Wild Italy: Top Predators
programme.desc: No one expected the outcome of introducing seven Slovenian bears into Trentino's Adamello woods.

programme.title: Wild Italy: Top Predators
programme.desc: Two eagles struggle to survive and produce offspring to maintain their species.

programme.title: Germany's Underworld
programme.sub-title: From the Alps to the River Rhine
programme.desc: In this episode, we meet powerful hunters like the pike and sinister meat-eating plants like the bladderwort.

programme.title: Germany's Underworld
programme.sub-title: From the Harz Mountain to the North Sea
programme.desc: In this episode meet the ancient Atlantic sturgeon, the ground beetle, the sea eagle, and a family of beavers.

programme.title: Shetland's Otter, the tale of a Draatsi Family
programme.desc: Film depicts the harsh survival struggles of otters in Europe's highest-density region, the Shetlands.

programme.title: Trees Of Life
programme.desc: See how humble plants triggered Earth’s greatest transformation

programme.title: Storks - A Village Rooftop Saga
programme.desc: Ruhstadt, Germany, hosts Germany's largest white stork colony, despite facing damage to roads, roofs

programme.title: Wild Carpathia: Seasons of Change
programme.desc: Follow the changing seasons of Romania, from the ripe glory of autumn through the depths of winter.

programme.title: Band Of Bears
programme.desc: Follow three playful bear cubs as they explore Scandinavia’s wild forests, home to moose, wolves, and rare flying squirrels!

programme.title: Band Of Bears
programme.desc: Deep in the snowy forests of Scandinavia, a mother bear raises her cubs, guiding them through their first year in the wild.

programme.title: Wild Faces Of Switzerland
programme.desc: We visit the Jura Mountains, vast and mysterious

programme.title: Wild Faces Of Switzerland
programme.desc: Graubünden in winter means there is a struggle for survival because of the cold.

programme.title: Wild Italy: Top Predators
programme.desc: The wolf is performing a remarkable comeback in the mountains of Italy.

programme.title: Wild Italy: Top Predators
programme.desc: No one expected the outcome of introducing seven Slovenian bears into Trentino's Adamello woods.

programme.title: Germany's Underworld
programme.sub-title: From the Alps to the River Rhine
programme.desc: In this episode, we meet powerful hunters like the pike and sinister meat-eating plants like the bladderwort.
